title: Requirements Workshop Type
content: Purpose: elicit and validate requirements. When to use: project kickoff; new features; unclear scope. Typical output: prioritized requirement list; process flow diagrams; action items. Best when stakeholders need structured dialogue to surface conflicting needs and agree on scope.

title: JAD Joint Application Design Workshop Type
content: Purpose: accelerated design with all parties present simultaneously. When to use: complex features needing rapid consensus; decisions requiring both business and technical input. Reduces back-and-forth cycles. Includes business users; IT architects; developers; and BA facilitator in same session.

title: Brainstorming Workshop Type
content: Purpose: generate ideas creatively without criticism. When to use: innovation initiatives; new product development; problem-solving sessions where conventional thinking is stuck. Separates idea generation from evaluation. Quantity of ideas over quality in generation phase.

title: Design Thinking Workshop Type
content: Purpose: human-centered problem solving through empathy and iteration. When to use: UX improvements; new user experiences; wicked problems. Five phases: Empathize; Define; Ideate; Prototype; Test. Full day format recommended for complete cycle.

title: Process Mapping Workshop Type
content: Purpose: document current state and design future state processes. When to use: process improvement initiatives; system replacement; identifying bottlenecks and waste. Output: as-is and to-be process maps in BPMN or swimlane format with pain points documented.
tags: process-mapping workshop type bpmn as-is to-be

title: Prioritization Workshop Type
content: Purpose: rank requirements or features collaboratively. When to use: backlog grooming; release planning; trade-off decisions with multiple competing requirements. Techniques: MoSCoW; dot voting; WSJF; dollar test. Requires product owner or sponsor authority to finalize decisions.

title: Review Walkthrough Workshop Type
content: Purpose: validate documents with stakeholders to gain formal approval. When to use: SRS review; requirements sign-off; architecture review. Structured inspection process. Participants review document sections and record issues defects and questions for resolution before approval.

title: Workshop Lifecycle - Phase 1 Plan
content: Plan phase 2 weeks before: define clear objectives and success criteria; identify required attendees and their roles; choose appropriate techniques for objectives; create detailed agenda with time boxes; book room or virtual platform; identify pre-work needed from participants. Planning quality determines workshop success.

title: Workshop Lifecycle - Phase 2 Prepare
content: Prepare phase 1 week before: send calendar invites with agenda attached; distribute pre-read materials; confirm attendance and handle conflicts; prepare all physical or digital materials; brief scribe and co-facilitator on their roles; test all technology including video and collaboration tools.

title: Workshop Lifecycle - Phase 3 Execute
content: Execute phase during workshop: facilitate according to agenda with flexibility; timekeep and signal transitions; capture outputs on whiteboard or digital board; manage group dynamics and ensure participation; drive toward objectives; handle conflicts using SKILL-06 techniques if escalation needed.
tags: workshop lifecycle execute facilitate timekeep dynamics

title: Workshop Lifecycle - Phase 4 Follow-Up
content: Follow-up phase 1 to 2 days after: document and distribute workshop summary; share action items with owners and due dates; distribute photos of whiteboard outputs or links to digital board; schedule follow-up sessions for open items; review participant feedback for improvement; archive materials.

title: Workshop Charter Template
content: Charter sections: Basic Information with name type date duration location facilitator and scribe; Objectives listing 3 specific measurable outcomes; Scope with in-scope topics and out-of-scope parking lot items; Participants table with name role contribution and required or optional; Pre-Work list; Deliverables list; Success Criteria.

title: Half-Day Requirements Workshop Agenda 4 Hours
content: Agenda: 9:00-9:15 Welcome and objectives; 9:15-9:45 Context setting by project sponsor; 9:45-10:30 Current state discovery with process mapping and sticky notes; 10:30-10:45 Break; 10:45-11:30 Requirements elicitation with brainstorming and affinity grouping; 11:30-12:15 Prioritization with MoSCoW or dot voting; 12:15-12:45 Review and next steps; 12:45-13:00 Feedback and close.

title: Full-Day Design Thinking Workshop Agenda 6 Hours
content: Agenda: 9:00-9:30 Setup and Design Thinking introduction; 9:30-10:30 Empathize with persona review journey mapping and empathy mapping; 10:30-10:45 Break; 10:45-11:30 Define with affinity mapping HMW questions and POV statement; 11:30-12:30 Ideate with Crazy 8s round robin and dot voting; 12:30-13:30 Lunch; 13:30-14:30 Prototype; 14:30-15:00 Test; 15:15-16:00 Synthesize; 16:00-16:30 Close.

title: Classic Brainstorming Facilitation Technique
content: Process: facilitator poses question; all ideas called out and scribe captures; NO criticism during idea generation phase; build on others ideas using yes-and; time-box 10 to 15 minutes. Rule: separate idea generation from evaluation completely. Quantity before quality. Good for verbal extroverted groups.

title: Silent Brainstorm Brainwriting Technique
content: Process: each person writes ideas on sticky notes silently with one idea per note; 5 to 10 minutes of silent writing; then share and group ideas together. Best for: introverts who think before speaking; avoiding groupthink where dominant voices suppress quieter ideas; remote teams using digital boards.

title: Round Robin Facilitation Technique
content: Process: go around room and each person gives one idea per turn; can pass if stuck; continue rounds until ideas are exhausted. Best for: ensuring equal participation from all attendees; preventing dominant voices; including quieter team members. Works well after initial brainstorm to capture remaining ideas.

title: Crazy 8s Ideation Technique
content: Process: fold paper into 8 sections; 1 minute per section to sketch one idea; forces rapid ideation without overthinking; 8 ideas in 8 minutes. Best for: design solutions and UI concepts; breaking creative blocks; teams that need to generate many diverse options quickly before converging.
tags: crazy-8s ideation technique design rapid sketching

title: Reverse Brainstorming Technique
content: Process: ask how could we make this problem WORSE; generate all the bad ideas freely; then flip each bad idea to reveal its positive solution counterpart. Best for: stuck teams who cannot generate positive ideas; identifying hidden risks; unconventional problems requiring creative reframing.

title: Starbursting Exploration Technique
content: Process: place idea or concept in center of star; generate questions using six points: Who; What; When; Where; Why; How. Best for: thoroughly exploring and refining concepts before committing to them; uncovering hidden assumptions; requirements discovery for complex or novel features.

title: Affinity Mapping Convergence Technique
content: Process: spread all sticky notes on wall or table; silently group similar items together without discussion; name each group with a category or theme label; then discuss and refine groupings as a group. Time 15 to 20 minutes. Reveals natural categories and themes in large volumes of unstructured input.

title: Dot Voting Prioritization Technique
content: Process: each participant gets 3 to 5 dots; vote by placing dots on preferred items; can place multiple dots on one item to show strong preference; count votes and rank by total dots received. Time 5 to 10 minutes. Variations: color dots for like concerns and questions; weighted dots for senior stakeholders.

title: Dollar Test Buy-a-Feature Technique
content: Process: give each participant 100 fake dollars; features have prices based on implementation effort; participants buy features they want most; participants can pool money to afford expensive features; reveals true priorities and forces explicit trade-offs. Time 20 to 30 minutes. Very effective for product prioritization.

title: 2x2 Matrix Prioritization Technique
content: Process: define two axes such as Value versus Effort; place each idea on the matrix through group discussion; debate placement to surface assumptions; focus on high-value low-effort quadrant for quick wins. Time 15 to 20 minutes. Axes variations: Impact vs Feasibility; Risk vs Reward; Strategic vs Tactical.

title: Fist of Five Consensus Check Technique
content: Process: propose a decision; everyone shows fingers simultaneously to prevent anchoring; 5 means strongly agree and will champion; 4 means agree; 3 means can live with it; 2 means have concerns needing discussion; 1 means strongly disagree and will block. Address all 1s and 2s before proceeding. Time 2 to 5 minutes per decision.

title: Opening Script for Workshop Facilitation
content: Opening script elements: welcome and introduce facilitator and scribe; quick introductions with name role and one hoped outcome; share 2 to 3 session objectives; agree on ground rules: one conversation at a time; all ideas welcome; stay present; timebox trust the process; parking lot for off-scope topics; invite additions before starting.

title: Closing Script for Workshop Facilitation
content: Closing script elements: summarize key outcomes with specific numbers such as X requirements identified; confirm action items with owner and due date reading each aloud; next steps including summary distribution date and follow-up session; read parking lot items and assign owners; request quick feedback on what worked and what to improve.

title: Managing Dominator Difficult Dynamic
content: Dominator monopolizes discussion. Techniques: thank the person then redirect to others by name - let us hear from others; use structured round robin to force equal participation; physically move to other side of room and invite quieter participants. Preserve dominator relationship while creating space for other voices.

title: Managing Silent Participant Difficult Dynamic
content: Silent participant not contributing. Techniques: before moving on invite by name referencing their expertise - you have worked closely with this area any thoughts; use silent brainstorming writing technique to capture their input without speaking pressure; speak to them during break and ask if they are comfortable with the format.

title: Managing HIPPO Effect in Workshops
content: HIPPO is Highest Paid Person Opinion dominating discussion. Techniques: use anonymous voting or silent brainstorming so status does not influence votes; ask senior person to speak LAST after all others have contributed; frame as let us hear from the team first then senior person can add perspective. Protects diverse input.

title: Parking Lot Management Technique
content: Parking lot captures important but off-scope topics during workshop. Process: when tangent arises say that is important let us capture it in parking lot and come back; write item on dedicated section of board; revisit at close and assign owner and follow-up date for each item. Prevents scope creep while respecting all input.

title: Managing Workshop Conflict Technique
content: When conflict erupts between participants: summarize both perspectives neutrally stating Person A believes X and Person B believes Y then say both are valid; ask what do you both agree on to find common ground; offer to take offline if resolution needs more time; load SKILL-06 Harvard negotiation method if conflict escalates beyond facilitation.

title: Ground Rules Template for Workshops
content: Standard workshop ground rules: One conversation at a time to respect each speaker; All ideas welcome with no criticism during brainstorming; Stay present with phones on silent and laptops closed unless needed; Timebox trust that facilitator will keep on track; Parking lot for off-scope topics captured for later. Ask participants to add their own rules.

title: Icebreaker Techniques for Workshop Opening
content: Four icebreakers: Two Truths and a Lie 5-10 min for new teams; Rose Bud Thorn 5 min sharing positive opportunity and challenge for context setting; One Word Check-in 2-3 min for tight timeframes; Superhero Power 5 min asking what superpower for this project which reveals hidden concerns. Choose based on group familiarity and time available.

title: Energizer Techniques for Workshop Re-engagement
content: Four energizers after breaks: Stand and Stretch 2 min for physical reset; Quick Poll 3 min asking scale 1-5 how clear are you on X using raised hands; Pair Share 5 min turn to neighbor discuss one key insight from last session; Gallery Walk 10 min post outputs on walls and participants comment with sticky notes.

title: Virtual Workshop Best Practices
content: Tools needed: Video platform Zoom Teams or Meet; Whiteboard Miro Mural FigJam; Polling Mentimeter or Slido; visible timer. Key practices: more frequent breaks every 45-60 min; shorter sessions max 3 hours; poll every 15-20 min; use chat for parallel input; call on people by name; have co-facilitator manage chat; encourage video on.

title: Workshop Materials Checklist In-Person
content: In-person materials: sticky notes multiple colors; thick markers multiple colors; flip chart paper or whiteboard; tape for posting on walls; dot stickers for voting; visible timer; name tags; printed agenda per participant; printed pre-read materials; snacks and refreshments; projector and screen; laptop with presentation; camera to capture outputs.

title: Master Facilitation Checklist - All Phases
content: Checklist phases: 2 weeks before define objectives invite participants book room draft agenda; 1 week before send invite with agenda distribute pre-read confirm attendance prepare materials brief scribe test technology; Day before final check send reminder review timing prepare parking lot set up room; Day of arrive early set up greet participants; During start on time follow agenda manage dynamics capture outputs; 24-48 hours after send summary distribute action items schedule follow-ups.

title: Workshop Outcomes Documentation Template
content: Outcomes document structure: Workshop name date attendees; Objectives achieved or partially achieved; Key decisions made with rationale; Requirements or ideas captured organized by theme; Action items table with item owner and due date; Parking lot items with assigned owner; Next steps and follow-up session date; Feedback summary plus delta format.

title: How Might We HMW Question Technique
content: HMW is used in Design Thinking Define phase. Process: take insights from empathy research; reframe each pain point as How Might We question; example: users abandon checkout becomes How Might We make checkout feel effortless. HMW questions frame problems as opportunities without presupposing solutions. Generate 5-10 HMW statements then vote on most impactful.

title: Managing Energy Low and Disengaged Participants
content: When energy drops or participants disengage: call a quick 2-minute stretch break; change activity format from passive discussion to active hands-on exercise; use an energizer technique; do explicit check-in asking on scale 1-5 how engaged are you and what would help. Engagement is facilitator responsibility not just participant responsibility.

title: Managing Running Over Time in Workshops
content: When running over time: announce with 10 minutes left moving to next activity; ask can we agree to simplified approach and take detailed discussion offline; have contingency plan knowing which agenda items can be cut without losing core objectives. Prevention: time-box each section on agenda and track visibly with timer participants can see.

title: Workshop Decision-Making Framework Selection
content: Choose decision technique based on context: Dot Voting for quick preference indication with large groups; Fist of Five for checking consensus and blocking issues; Dollar Test for hard trade-off decisions with budget analogy; 2x2 Matrix for strategic positioning decisions; MoSCoW for requirement prioritization with clear must-have definition. Match technique to decision type and group size.
